title: Usage
text:
using Pkg
Pkg.add(url = "https://github.com/fesmc/PlasticIceSheet.jl")
The core package depends only on LinearAlgebra. Reverse-mode differentiation and NetCDF I/O are loaded on demand through package extensions (below).

title: Usage
text:
using Pkg
Pkg.add(url = "https://github.com/fesmc/PlasticIceSheet.jl")
The core package depends only on LinearAlgebra. Reverse-mode differentiation and NetCDF I/O are loaded on demand through package extensions (below).

title: Usage
text:
Solving
using PlasticIceSheet

E, H = solve(bed, τ, mask; dx, dy, mode = :hj)







argument
meaning




bed
bed elevation B (m), nx × ny matrix


τ
basal shear stress (Pa); a scalar is broadcast to a uniform field


mask
true where grounded ice exists; the margin is the mask boundary


dx, dy
grid spacings (m)



Key options (see the docstring for the rest):

mode — :hj (default, full Hamilton–Jacobi over arbitrary relief) or :flat (flat-bed eikonal, cheapest; exact when relief ≪ thickness).
params — a PlasticParams (densities, gravity, sea level, H_min).
n_outer, outer_tol, relax — :hj damped fixed-point controls. relax ∈ (0,1] is the under-relaxation factor (default 0.5); lower is more robust on steep beds but slower.

Returns (E, H); ice-free cells have H = 0, E = bed.

Diagnostics
ice_volume(H, dx, dy)            # m³
ice_area(mask, dx, dy)           # m²
surface_misfit(E, E_obs, mask)   # mean-squared surface misfit over ice

title: Usage
text:
Inverting for \tau
The solver is differentiable with respect to \tau. See Algorithms §7 for the theory.

Forward mode — scalar / low-dimensional \tau
using ForwardDiff
loss(τ) = surface_misfit(first(solve(bed, τ, mask; dx, dy)), E_obs, mask)
g = ForwardDiff.derivative(loss, 1.0e5)


Reverse mode — full per-cell \tau field
Load the extension triggers, then differentiate differentiable_thickness with any reverse-mode AD:
using ImplicitDifferentiation, ADTypes, ForwardDiff, Zygote

loss(τ) = surface_misfit(differentiable_thickness(τ, bed, mask; dx, dy) .+ bed, E_obs, mask)
g = Zygote.gradient(loss, τ)[1]      # gradient w.r.t. the whole τ field
A complete, runnable Adam-based recovery of a spatially-varying \tau from an observed surface is in examples/invert_tau.jl. It drives the misfit down by four orders of magnitude and recovers the two-region truth to within a fraction of a kPa.






Tip



The default reverse-mode linear solver is direct (robust for modest grids). For very large fields, pass an iterative solver — see the differentiable_thickness docstring.

title: Usage
text:
NetCDF I/O
using NCDatasets

inp = load_plastic_inputs("inputs.nc")             # (; bed, τ, mask, dx, dy, x, y)
E, H = solve(inp.bed, inp.τ, inp.mask; inp.dx, inp.dy)
save_reconstruction("out.nc", E, H; x = inp.x, y = inp.y)

title: Usage
text:
Caveats

Steady-state geometry only — biased thick/peaked where the real ice was streaming or dynamic (low effective driving stress the plastic assumption cannot see).
\tau carries essentially all the uncertainty. For a first guess a constant (~50–100 kPa) or a bed-keyed value is fine; do not over-interpret the result as a constrained reconstruction.
Very steep beds (relief approaching the ice thickness over a few cells) may not converge in :hj — see Algorithms §3.

title: Algorithms
text: This page explains, end to end, how PlasticIceSheet.jl turns a margin, a bed, and a basal-shear-stress field into an ice sheet — and how it differentiates the result with respect to the shear stress.

title: Algorithms
text:
1. The governing equation
The reconstruction assumes perfectly plastic, steady-state ice: the gravitational driving stress is everywhere balanced by the basal shear stress \tau. For an ice surface E, bed B, and thickness H = E - B, this balance is

|\nabla E| = \frac{\tau}{\rho_i\, g\, (E - B)} = \frac{\tau}{\rho_i\, g\, H},
\tag{1}
with \rho_i the ice density and g gravity. The right-hand side is a slope (dimensionless). Equation 1 is a static Hamilton–Jacobi equation: the surface is built inward from the margin, where H \to 0. Its upwind (viscosity) solution automatically yields the correct envelope wherever ice converges, so ice divides, domes, and merging ice caps appear without any explicit flowline or saddle handling.
This is the same physics as ICESHEET 1.0, but solved on a grid rather than by tracing characteristics.

title: Algorithms
text:
2. Solve for H^2, not E
Near the margin H \to 0, so the driving slope \tau/(\rho_i g H) in Equation 1 diverges. Discretising Equation 1 for E directly is therefore inaccurate near the margin and biases the whole reconstruction thin. The fix is a change of variable.
On a flat bed, Equation 1 reads |\nabla H| = \tau/(\rho_i g H). Multiplying by 2H and using \nabla(H^2) = 2H\,\nabla H,

\bigl|\nabla (H^2)\bigr| = \frac{2\tau}{\rho_i\, g}.
\tag{2}
The singularity is gone: w \equiv H^2 satisfies a plain eikonal |\nabla w| = G with a finite right-hand side G = 2\tau/(\rho_i g), and the boundary condition w = 0 at the margin is regular. Both solver modes work in w = H^2; the thickness is recovered as H = \sqrt{w} and the surface as E = H + B.

:flat mode
:flat solves Equation 2 exactly and drapes the result on the bed. It is the cheapest mode and is appropriate whenever bed relief is small compared with ice thickness (continental interiors). ICESHEET’s own sensitivity tests indicate the bed-gradient terms are second-order except in mountainous terrain.

title: Algorithms
text:
3. :hj mode — the bed-gradient correction
Over significant relief the bed slope \nabla B enters the flux. Writing \nabla E = \nabla H + \nabla B in Equation 1 and carrying the algebra through in the w = H^2 variable gives, exactly in one dimension,

|\nabla w| = \sqrt{\,G^2 \;-\; 4\sqrt{w}\,(\nabla B \cdot \nabla w)\;-\;4\,w\,|\nabla B|^2\,},
\qquad G = \frac{2\tau}{\rho_i g}.
\tag{3}
Two properties make Equation 3 the right object to solve:

when \nabla B = 0 it reduces to the flat eikonal Equation 2, so :hj and :flat agree exactly on a flat bed;
it is written in w = H^2, so it inherits the regular margin behaviour of Equation 2.

The effective right-hand side depends on \nabla w (through the \nabla B\cdot\nabla w term), so Equation 3 is not a pure eikonal. It is solved by an outer fixed-point iteration: freeze the right-hand side from the current w, solve the resulting constant-RHS eikonal for a candidate w_c, and update.

Damping is required
The naive update w \leftarrow \text{Eikonal}\!\left(\text{RHS}(w)\right) is not contractive — over real bed relief it oscillates indefinitely. The solver therefore uses an under-relaxed (damped) Picard iteration,

w^{(k+1)} = (1-\alpha)\, w^{(k)} + \alpha\, \text{Eikonal}\!\left(\text{RHS}(w^{(k)})\right),

with relaxation factor \alpha = \texttt{relax} (default 0.5). Damping converges the iteration to the same fixed point — i.e. to a vanishing residual (§6) — which is also what makes the implicit-differentiation gradient (§7) exact. Smaller \alpha is more robust on steep beds but needs more iterations; beds whose relief approaches the ice thickness over a few cells may not converge at all (lower relax, raise n_outer, refine the grid, or fall back to :flat).

title: Algorithms
text:
4. The Godunov update
The eikonal |\nabla u| = F is discretised cell-by-cell with the first-order Godunov upwind scheme. At a cell with grid spacings \Delta x, \Delta y, let a and b be the smaller of the two neighbour values along each axis:

a = \min(u_{i-1,j},\, u_{i+1,j}), \qquad b = \min(u_{i,j-1},\, u_{i,j+1}).

The updated value is the smallest u satisfying

\left(\frac{(u-a)_+}{\Delta x}\right)^{2} + \left(\frac{(u-b)_+}{\Delta y}\right)^{2} = F^2,
\qquad (\cdot)_+ = \max(\cdot, 0).

In practice: try the two-sided quadratic; accept it only if the result exceeds both neighbours (both upwind terms active); otherwise take the smaller one-sided update \min(a + F\,\Delta x,\; b + F\,\Delta y). This is godunov_eikonal, a pure function with no mutation or indexing — which is what lets the autodiff back ends differentiate it directly.

title: Algorithms
text:
5. Fast sweeping
The discrete eikonal is solved by the fast-sweeping method: Gauss–Seidel updates that keep the monotone minimum,

u_{i,j} \leftarrow \min\!\bigl(u_{i,j},\; \text{Godunov update}\bigr),

applied over the four diagonal traversal orderings (\nearrow, \nwarrow, \swarrow,
\searrow) in rotation. Because each ordering captures characteristics flowing in one quadrant of directions, a handful of sweeps converges the whole field in O(N) work, for any geometry. Interior cells start at +\infty; ice-free cells hold their boundary value (§6) so neighbour look-ups only ever read the working array.

title: Algorithms
text:
6. Boundary conditions
The margin is the interface between mask (grounded ice) and ice-free cells. Ice-free cells carry the Dirichlet value:

Land margin (B \ge sea level): H = 0, i.e. w = 0.
Marine margin (B < sea level): the grounding line sits at the flotation thickness — the thickness whose weight balances the local water column, 
H_{\text{flot}} = \frac{\rho_w}{\rho_i}\,\bigl(\text{sea level} - B\bigr), \qquad w = H_{\text{flot}}^2,
 reproducing ICESHEET’s marine grounding-line treatment. The eikonal then grows the ice inward from that thickness. No separate slope-limiting pre-pass is needed: the maximum surface slope is the plastic constraint the eikonal enforces.

title: Algorithms
text:
7. Differentiating with respect to \tau
Because the solver is a pure function of \tau, an objective on the output (a misfit to an observed surface, a target volume, …) is differentiable — enabling basal-shear-stress inversion.

Forward mode
For a scalar \tau or a low-dimensional parameterization, ForwardDiff differentiates straight through solve. Cost scales with the number of parameters.


Reverse mode (implicit differentiation)
For a full per-cell \tau field, differentiating through the sweeps would be wasteful. Instead, the converged field satisfies an optimality condition — the Godunov residual

r_{i,j}(w, \tau) = w_{i,j} - \bigl(\text{Godunov update at } (i,j)\bigr) = 0

on ice cells (and w - \text{bc} = 0 off-ice). By the implicit function theorem, for a loss L(w),

\frac{\partial L}{\partial \tau}
= -\,\frac{\partial L}{\partial w}\left(\frac{\partial r}{\partial w}\right)^{-1}\frac{\partial r}{\partial \tau},

so the gradient costs a single linear solve with the residual Jacobian, independent of the number of sweeps or outer iterations and scalable to a full field. The forward fast sweep is treated as a black box; only the residual is differentiated (with ForwardDiff internally). This is wired up with ImplicitDifferentiation.jl in a package extension, so any reverse-mode AD (e.g. Zygote) obtains the gradient.
The :flat gradient is exact to machine precision; the :hj gradient is exact once the damped forward solve (§3) has driven the residual to \approx 0.






NoteNaN hygiene



\sqrt{w} has an infinite derivative at w=0. Wherever \sqrt{w} is differentiated it is floored at H_{\min}^2, because the unguarded \sqrt{0} derivative produces \infty\cdot 0
= \text{NaN} in the reverse pass (forward mode dodges this by branch selection).

title: Algorithms
text:
8. What was deliberately dropped
Relative to ICESHEET 1.0, the grid Hamilton–Jacobi solver makes the following machinery unnecessary: method-of-characteristics flowline tracing, crossover detection (the motorcycle-graph algorithm), saddle/dome detection and polygon splitting, the GIA / sea-level iteration loop, and the GMT projection/preprocessing pipeline. See the Design notes for the full accounting.

title: PlasticIceSheet.jl
text:
PlasticIceSheet.jl reconstructs a perfectly-plastic, steady-state ice sheet from three inputs — a grounded-ice margin/mask, a bed topography B, and a basal shear stress field \tau — and returns a glaciologically plausible ice surface E and thickness H. There is no time-stepping, no climate, and no ice dynamics.
It is meant as a complementary first-guess tool next to a full dynamic model (e.g. Yelmo) and an independent GIA / sea-level solver: to seed a spin-up, force a surface-mass-balance model, or serve as a relaxation target.

title: PlasticIceSheet.jl
text:
The idea in one equation
The ice surface satisfies the perfectly-plastic balance — driving stress equals basal shear stress everywhere:

|\nabla E| = \frac{\tau}{\rho_i\, g\, (E - B)} = \frac{\tau}{\rho_i\, g\, H}, \qquad H = E - B .

This is a static Hamilton–Jacobi (eikonal) equation. Solving it with an upwind grid scheme means saddles, domes, and coalescing ice caps emerge automatically from the viscosity solution — there is no flowline tracing, crossover detection, or saddle bookkeeping. See Algorithms for the full derivation and numerics.

title: PlasticIceSheet.jl
text:
Quick start
using PlasticIceSheet

nx, ny = 100, 100
dx = dy = 2000.0                                   # grid spacing (m)
bed  = zeros(nx, ny)                               # bed elevation B (m)
mask = [hypot(i-50.5, j-50.5) <= 40 for i in 1:nx, j in 1:ny]
τ    = 1.0e5                                       # basal shear stress (Pa)

E, H = solve(bed, τ, mask; dx, dy, mode = :hj)     # :hj (default) or :flat
A full Hamilton–Jacobi solve on a 300×300 grid takes ~0.1 s. The basal shear stress \tau is differentiable, enabling gradient-based inversion.

title: PlasticIceSheet.jl
text:
Origin
This is a clean-room simplification and modernization of ICESHEET 1.0 (Gowan et al. 2016, GMD; source). The core physics — one published equation — is retained; the ~2,600-line recursive flowline / crossover / saddle machinery is replaced by a grid eikonal solver on the order of a couple hundred lines. The Design notes record what was kept, simplified, and dropped, and why.
PlasticIceSheet.jl is MIT-licensed.

title: PlasticIceSheet.jl — design & plan
text:
A small, AD-friendly Julia package that reconstructs a perfectly-plastic, steady-state ice-sheet surface from three inputs — a grounded-ice margin/mask, a basal shear stress field τ(x,y), and a bed topography B(x,y) — by solving the governing equation as a static Hamilton–Jacobi (eikonal-type) problem on a grid.
It is intended as a complementary first-guess tool alongside a full dynamic model (Yelmo) and an independent 3-D GIA / sea-level solver — e.g. to seed spin-up, to provide an elevation field for an SMB model, or as a relaxation target. It is not a dynamic model and carries no transient, thermomechanics, or ice-shelf physics.


This package is a deliberate simplification and modernization of ICESHEET 1.0 by Evan J. Gowan et al.:

Source code (Fortran): https://github.com/evangowan/icesheet
Local copy analyzed: ~/models/icesheet
Description paper: Gowan, E. J., Tregoning, P., Purcell, A., Lea, J., Fransner, O. J., Noormets, R., and Dowdeswell, J. A. (2016). ICESHEET 1.0: a program to produce paleo-ice sheet reconstructions with minimal assumptions. Geoscientific Model Development, 9(5), 1673–1682. https://doi.org/10.5194/gmd-9-1673-2016 (local: ~/models/icesheet/gmd-9-1673-2016.xml)
v2.0 / PaleoMIST application: Gowan et al. (2021), Nature Communications 12, 1199. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-021-21469-w

ICESHEET is GPL-3.0. This is a clean-room reimplementation of the physics (one published equation, below); none of the Fortran source is copied. License choice for this package is TBD (see Open questions).



The entire scientific content of ICESHEET is one equation — the perfectly-plastic, steady-state ice surface, where driving stress balances basal shear stress everywhere (paper Eq. 5):
|∇E| = τ / (ρ_i g (E − B)) = τ / (ρ_i g H),    H = E − B ≥ 0
with E the ice surface elevation, B the bed, H the thickness, τ the basal shear stress, ρ_i ice density, g gravity. Boundary condition: H = 0 (E = B) at the margin, plus a marine flotation thickness H = −B(1 − ρ_w/ρ_i) where the grounded margin sits below sea level.
This is a static Hamilton–Jacobi equation. The viscosity solution of an upwind grid scheme automatically produces the correct surface where flow would converge — so saddles, domes, and coalescing ice caps emerge for free, with no flowline tracing.



:flat — flat-bed eikonal (simple case). Neglect bed slope in the flux (|∇E| ≈ |∇H|). Substituting u = H² gives a textbook eikonal:
|∇u| = 2τ / (ρ_i g),     u = 0 at the margin
Solve, then H = √u, E = H + B. Exact eikonal; ideal when bed relief ≪ ice thickness (continental interiors). The paper’s own sensitivity tests indicate the bed-gradient terms are second-order except in mountainous terrain.
:hj — full Hamilton–Jacobi (default). Solve directly for E:
|∇E| = τ / (ρ_i g (E − B))
The right-hand side depends on E at the node (f = f(x, E), monotone decreasing in E), so each Godunov node update is a local nonlinear solve (a few inner iterations / local Newton). Correct over arbitrary bed relief.





Fast sweeping (Zhao 2005): Gauss–Seidel with the Godunov upwind Hamiltonian, alternating the four diagonal sweep orderings, iterated to a tolerance on the max update. O(N) per sweep, a handful of sweeps to converge.
Godunov update at node (i,j) with spacing h, neighbor minima a, b:

if |a − b| ≥ F h: u = min(a, b) + F h
else: u = (a + b + √(2 F² h² − (a − b)²)) / 2 where F = 2τ/(ρ_i g) (flat mode) or the E-dependent local solve (HJ mode).


This replaces the ~2,600-line recursive flowline/crossover/saddle machinery of ICESHEET (find_flowline_fisher_adaptive_4.f90) with a kernel on the order of ~150–250 lines.




Given the intended use (first guess; external GIA; Yelmo for dynamics), we drop:

Method-of-characteristics flowline tracing and all of its bookkeeping: crossover detection / motorcycle-graph algorithm, saddle & dome detection, polygon splitting, recursion, adaptive contour resampling. Subsumed by the grid HJ solver.
The GIA / sea-level iteration loop (CALSEA/SELEN coupling, global/deform/, selen_format.sh, …). The package consumes a bed B; the user owns GIA.
The GMT projection/preprocessing pipeline (run.sh, prepare_icesheet.sh, create_ss_grid.f90, shapefile→τ domains, reduce_dem, nearest_int, bicubic, diff_map) and the hand-rolled GMT binary-record grid reader (grids.f90). Replaced by in-memory arrays now, NetCDF I/O via a package extension later.
The offshore boundary Nye-smoothing pre-pass — the HJ scheme enforces the maximum surface slope intrinsically.
The iterative τ-tuning shell loop (adjust_ss) — replaced by gradient-based optimization of τ enabled by AD (see below).





Inputs: grounded-ice mask, bed B, shear-stress field τ (or a constant / a low-dimensional parameterization).
Plastic physics + marine flotation BC at the grounding line.
Gridded outputs: surface E, thickness H, plus reductions (volume, area).
AD-friendliness: the solver is written so that a scalar loss on the output (e.g. misfit to a target surface, or to Yelmo) can be differentiated w.r.t. τ, enabling gradient-based inversion of basal shear stress — the modern replacement for ICESHEET’s manual domain tuning.





Steady-state geometry only; biased thick/peaked where the real ice was streaming or dynamic (low effective driving stress the plastic assumption can’t see).
τ is the free field carrying essentially all the uncertainty. For a first guess a constant (~50–100 kPa) or a bed-keyed value is fine; don’t over-interpret as a constrained reconstruction.





AD backend & strategy (forward-through-solver vs. implicit/adjoint at the fixed point; Enzyme vs. ForwardDiff vs. Zygote) — depends on whether τ is a full per-cell field or a low-dimensional parameterization. To be decided before the core lands.
License (ICESHEET is GPL-3.0; this is a clean-room physics reimplementation).
NetCDF I/O as a package extension (ext/).





Both modes solve for w = H², not the surface E directly: the driving slope τ/(ρ_i g H) blows up at the margin (H→0) and biases a direct-E solve low. :flat is the plain eikonal |∇w| = 2τ/(ρ_i g); :hj adds the bed-gradient correction.
:hj damping. The :hj effective RHS √(G² − 4√w(∇B·∇w) − 4w|∇B|²) carries ∇w, so “freeze RHS, re-solve” is not contractive — undamped it oscillates over real relief. It is solved by a damped (under-relaxed) Picard iteration (relax, default 0.5). Very steep beds (relief ≈ ice thickness over a few cells) may still not converge.
Reverse-mode AD (differentiable_thickness, extension keyed on ImplicitDifferentiation): implicit differentiation of the converged Godunov residual godunov_residual(w, τ) = 0. :flat gradients are exact to machine precision; :hj gradients are exact once the damped forward solve has driven the residual to ≈ 0 (hence the firmer n_outer/outer_tol defaults on that path). The forward fast sweep is a black box; only the residual is differentiated (ForwardDiff), so AD cost is independent of sweep/outer-iteration count and scales to a full per-cell τ field.
NaN hygiene: √w is floored at H_min² wherever it is differentiated (sqrt(0) has an infinite derivative that otherwise poisons the reverse pass).





Package scaffolding, plan doc
Core HJ / eikonal fast-sweeping solver (:flat, :hj)
Forward-mode (ForwardDiff) τ-gradients, validated vs finite differences
Reverse-mode (implicit-diff) full-field τ-gradients, validated vs ForwardDiff/FD
Tests: Nye analytic profile, flat≡hj on flat bed, residual convergence, AD
NetCDF I/O extension
τ-inversion example (examples/invert_tau.jl)
Next: implicit-diff iterative linear solver tuning for very large grids; better :hj convergence on steep beds (e.g. Anderson acceleration / a true HJ Godunov for the bed-coupled term); coordinate/projection handling for real datasets

text:
Given the intended use (first guess; external GIA; Yelmo for dynamics), we drop:

Method-of-characteristics flowline tracing and all of its bookkeeping: crossover detection / motorcycle-graph algorithm, saddle & dome detection, polygon splitting, recursion, adaptive contour resampling. Subsumed by the grid HJ solver.

title: PlasticIceSheet.jl — design & plan
text:
Both modes solve for w = H², not the surface E directly: the driving slope τ/(ρ_i g H) blows up at the margin (H→0) and biases a direct-E solve low. :flat is the plain eikonal |∇w| = 2τ/(ρ_i g); :hj adds the bed-gradient correction.
:hj damping. The :hj effective RHS √(G² − 4√w(∇B·∇w) − 4w|∇B|²) carries ∇w, so “freeze RHS, re-solve” is not contractive — undamped it oscillates over real relief. It is solved by a damped (under-relaxed) Picard iteration (relax, default 0.5). Very steep beds (relief ≈ ice thickness over a few cells) may still not converge.
Reverse-mode AD (differentiable_thickness, extension keyed on ImplicitDifferentiation): implicit differentiation of the converged Godunov residual godunov_residual(w, τ) = 0. :flat gradients are exact to machine precision; :hj gradients are exact once the damped forward solve has driven the residual to ≈ 0 (hence the firmer n_outer/outer_tol defaults on that path). The forward fast sweep is a black box; only the residual is differentiated (ForwardDiff), so AD cost is independent of sweep/outer-iteration count and scales to a full per-cell τ field.
NaN hygiene: √w is floored at H_min² wherever it is differentiated (sqrt(0) has an infinite derivative that otherwise poisons the reverse pass).

title: PlasticIceSheet.jl — design & plan
text:
Package scaffolding, plan doc
Core HJ / eikonal fast-sweeping solver (:flat, :hj)
Forward-mode (ForwardDiff) τ-gradients, validated vs finite differences
Reverse-mode (implicit-diff) full-field τ-gradients, validated vs ForwardDiff/FD
Tests: Nye analytic profile, flat≡hj on flat bed, residual convergence, AD
NetCDF I/O extension
τ-inversion example (examples/invert_tau.jl)
Next: implicit-diff iterative linear solver tuning for very large grids; better :hj convergence on steep beds (e.g. Anderson acceleration / a true HJ Godunov for the bed-coupled term); coordinate/projection handling for real datasets
